content: This technical reference document provides detailed information about ecec product specifications, characteristics, and performance parameters. Use this information to support your design and analysis activities.

Electrical characteristics are specified over the operating temperature range unless otherwise noted. Parameters are guaranteed by design, testing, or statistical analysis. Typical values represent the most likely parametric norm at 25°C.

Thermal characteristics require careful attention during system design. The junction-to-ambient thermal resistance depends on the mounting configuration, PCB copper area, and airflow conditions. Use thermal simulation tools to predict operating temperatures under actual conditions.

Reliability data is based on accelerated life testing and field failure analysis. Mean time between failures (MTBF) calculations follow industry-standard methodologies. Contact BeiLuo for detailed reliability reports and qualification data.

faeInsights.title: FAE - Crystal Applications
faeInsights.content: For crystal resonator selection, I always emphasize the importance of matching the load capacitance to your circuit design. The most common issue I see is customers using crystals with the wrong load capacitance, resulting in frequency errors. Over my 15 years of supporting timing applications, I have learned that proper load capacitance calculation is the single most important factor for successful crystal implementation. Many designers forget to account for stray capacitance from PCB traces and IC pins, which can add 2-5pF to the total load. This seemingly small difference can cause significant frequency errors. I always recommend using the formula CL = (C1 x C2)/(C1 + C2) + Cstray and measuring the actual frequency to verify proper operation.
faeInsights.logic: Load capacitance directly affects oscillation frequency. Mismatched CL causes frequency offset.
keyTakeaways: Match load capacitance to circuit design
Use industrial grade for harsh environments
faeInsights.insightLogic: These professional recommendations come from in-depth analysis of hundreds of successful product deployment projects across industries, covering the full lifecycle from concept design to mass production.

customerCases.challenge: Frequent crystal failures in high-vibration environment
customerCases.solution: Switched to HC-49/S industrial-grade crystals with proper mounting
customerCases.result: Zero failures over 3 years of operation
customerCases.feedback: The industrial-grade crystals solved our reliability issues completely.

customerCases.challenge: Frequency drift causing Bluetooth connectivity issues
customerCases.solution: Selected crystal with tighter tolerance and proper load capacitance
customerCases.feedback: Proper crystal selection eliminated our wireless connectivity problems.

faq.question: What is the difference between series and parallel resonant crystals?
faq.answer: Series resonant crystals are designed to operate at the series resonant frequency where impedance is minimum. Parallel resonant crystals are designed to operate with a specific load capacitance.
faq.decisionGuide: Most microcontroller applications use parallel resonant crystals.

faq.question: How do I calculate the correct load capacitors for my crystal?
faq.answer: Use the formula CL = (C1 x C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. For symmetric circuits where C1 = C2, this simplifies to CL = C1/2 + Cstray.
faq.decisionGuide: Account for stray capacitance of 2-5pF when calculating load capacitor values.

faq.question: What is the difference between AT-cut and tuning fork crystals?
faq.answer: AT-cut crystals use a quartz plate cut at a specific angle, providing excellent frequency stability in the MHz range. Tuning fork crystals operate at 32.768kHz for real-time clocks.
faq.decisionGuide: Use AT-cut for MHz frequencies, tuning fork for 32.768kHz RTC applications.

faq.question: How does temperature affect crystal frequency?
faq.answer: Crystal frequency varies with temperature in a predictable curve. AT-cut crystals have a characteristic S-curve with inflection points near room temperature.
faq.decisionGuide: For better temperature stability, consider TCXOs.

faq.question: What is crystal aging and how does it affect my application?
faq.answer: Aging is the long-term frequency drift of a crystal over time, typically ±3ppm per year. This is caused by mass transfer and stress relaxation in the crystal structure.
faq.decisionGuide: For applications requiring long-term accuracy, select crystals with lower aging specifications.

faq.question: Can I use any crystal with my microcontroller?
faq.answer: Not all crystals work with all microcontrollers. Check that frequency is within range, ESR is within limits, and load capacitance can be matched.
faq.decisionGuide: Always verify crystal specifications against microcontroller requirements.

faeInsights.title: FAE - PCB Design
faeInsights.content: The most common layout mistake I see is long traces between the crystal and microcontroller. Keep traces under 10mm for best performance. In my experience supporting hundreds of designs, poor PCB layout is the second most common cause of crystal oscillator problems after incorrect load capacitance. Long traces increase stray capacitance and inductance, which can shift the oscillation frequency and increase susceptibility to noise. I always recommend placing the crystal as close as possible to the microcontroller oscillator pins, ideally within 10mm. For high-frequency crystals above 50MHz, I suggest keeping traces under 5mm. Additionally, a solid ground plane under the crystal circuit is essential for providing a low-impedance return path and isolating the crystal from noise.
faeInsights.logic: Long traces increase stray capacitance and inductance, affecting oscillator performance.
faeInsights.insightLogic: These professional recommendations come from in-depth analysis of hundreds of successful product deployment projects across industries, covering the full lifecycle from concept design to mass production.

customerCases.challenge: Excessive jitter in precision timing circuit
customerCases.solution: Redesigned PCB layout with proper grounding and shorter traces
customerCases.feedback: Proper layout made the difference between failure and success.

customerCases.solution: Implemented guard ring and improved grounding
customerCases.result: EMI reduced by 40dB, clock stability achieved
customerCases.feedback: The guard ring technique solved our EMI problems.

faq.question: How close should the crystal be to the microcontroller?
faq.answer: The crystal should be placed as close as possible to the microcontroller oscillator pins, ideally within 10mm.
faq.decisionGuide: For high-frequency crystals, keep traces under 5mm when possible.

faq.question: Can I route other signals under the crystal?
faq.answer: Avoid routing other signals under or near the crystal circuit. Signals can couple noise into the oscillator circuit.
faq.decisionGuide: Maintain a keep-out area of at least 5mm around the crystal.

faq.question: Do I need a ground plane under the crystal?
faq.answer: Yes, a solid ground plane under the crystal circuit is recommended for low-impedance return path and noise isolation.
faq.decisionGuide: Avoid splitting the ground plane under the crystal.

faq.question: What is a guard ring and when should I use one?
faq.answer: A guard ring is a grounded trace surrounding sensitive circuits. For crystal circuits, it can help reduce EMI coupling.
faq.decisionGuide: Use a guard ring for high-frequency crystals or in noisy environments.

faq.question: How do I minimize stray capacitance in my crystal circuit?
faq.answer: Minimize stray capacitance by keeping traces short, using appropriate trace widths, and avoiding vias in crystal traces.
faq.decisionGuide: Account for stray capacitance of 2-5pF when calculating load capacitor values.

faq.question: Should I use a series resistor on the crystal output?
faq.answer: A series resistor can help reduce drive level and prevent overdriving the crystal. Typical values are 100-500 ohms.
faq.decisionGuide: Use if crystal is being overdriven or to reduce EMI.

articles.title: Crystal Oscillator vs Resonator: Which to Choose?
articles.titleCn: Crystal Oscillator vs Resonator: Which to Choose?
articles.summary: Comparison of crystal resonators and crystal oscillators to help select the right timing solution for your application.
articles.description: Comparison of crystal resonators and crystal oscillators to help select the right timing solution for your application.

faeInsights.title: FAE - Timing Solutions
faeInsights.content: I recommend crystal resonators for cost-sensitive applications with simple requirements. Use oscillators when you need guaranteed startup and better stability. After supporting thousands of designs over my career, I have developed a clear decision framework for choosing between resonators and oscillators. Crystal resonators are the best choice when cost is the primary concern and you have microcontroller with integrated oscillator circuit. They are also ideal when power consumption must be minimized. However, if you need guaranteed startup, better frequency stability, or are working in a wide temperature range, crystal oscillators are worth the additional cost. TCXOs should be used for precision applications like GPS and cellular communication where frequency accuracy directly affects system performance. Remember to factor in the total solution cost including external components and design time when making your decision.
faeInsights.logic: Resonators are cheaper but need external circuit. Oscillators cost more but work out of the box.
faeInsights.insightLogic: These professional recommendations come from in-depth analysis of hundreds of successful product deployment projects across industries, covering the full lifecycle from concept design to mass production.

customerCases.challenge: Unreliable crystal startup in battery-powered device
customerCases.solution: Switched to crystal oscillator for guaranteed startup
customerCases.feedback: The oscillator eliminated our startup failures completely.

customerCases.challenge: High BOM cost with oscillators for simple MCU clock
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with crystal resonator and proper load caps
customerCases.feedback: The resonator solution saved significant cost.

faq.question: Can I replace a crystal resonator with an oscillator in an existing design?
faq.answer: Yes, but modifications are required. Crystal oscillators have 3-4 pins and you'll need to remove external oscillator circuit components.
faq.decisionGuide: Check voltage levels and output type for compatibility.

faq.question: Why do oscillators cost more than resonators?
faq.answer: Oscillators contain additional active circuitry including amplifier, temperature compensation, and output driver.
faq.decisionGuide: The integrated circuit and assembly add to the cost but provide guaranteed performance.

faq.question: Do I need a TCXO or is a standard crystal sufficient?
faq.answer: Choose a TCXO when you need better than ±20-50ppm frequency stability over temperature.
faq.decisionGuide: GPS and cellular applications typically require TCXOs.

faq.question: What is the typical startup time difference?
faq.answer: Crystal resonators typically startup in 1-10ms. MEMS oscillators can startup in under 1ms.
faq.decisionGuide: For fast wakeup from sleep mode, consider startup time in your selection.

faq.question: Can I use a crystal oscillator with a microcontroller that has built-in oscillator circuit?
faq.answer: Yes, but you'll bypass the microcontroller's internal oscillator. Connect the oscillator output to the clock input pin.
faq.decisionGuide: Configure the microcontroller to use external clock mode.

faq.question: Which has lower power consumption?
faq.answer: Crystal resonators have lower power since they have no active circuitry. Oscillators consume 5-15mA typical.
faq.decisionGuide: For battery-powered devices, resonators offer lower power consumption.

articles.title: ECEC Crystal and Oscillator Design Guidelines
articles.summary: Comprehensive design guidelines for implementing ECEC crystal resonators and oscillators in your application, covering PCB layout, load capacitance calculation, and troubleshooting.
content: ## Introduction

This guide provides comprehensive design guidelines for implementing ECEC crystal resonators and oscillators in electronic systems. Following these recommendations will help ensure reliable oscillator startup, accurate frequency generation, and long-term stability.
## Crystal Resonator Design
### Load Capacitance Calculation
The load capacitance (CL) is critical for proper crystal operation. The crystal is designed to oscillate at the specified frequency only when loaded with the correct capacitance.
**Formula**: CL = (C1 × C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray
Where:
- C1, C2 = External load capacitors
- Cstray = Stray capacitance from PCB traces and IC pins (typically 3-5pF)
**Example calculation for 12pF crystal**:
- With Cstray = 4pF, required effective capacitance = 12 - 4 = 8pF
- Using C1 = C2: 8 = (C × C) / (C + C) = C/2
- Therefore C1 = C2 = 16pF
- Use standard 18pF capacitors (closest standard value)
### PCB Layout Guidelines
1. **Keep traces short**: Crystal traces should be as short as possible (<20mm ideal)
2. **Symmetric layout**: Keep C1 and C2 traces equal length
3. **Ground plane**: Use solid ground plane under crystal circuit
4. **Isolation**: Keep crystal away from high-frequency signals and switching regulators
5. **No vias under crystal**: Avoid vias in the crystal area to prevent mechanical stress
## Oscillator Design
### Power Supply Design
Crystal oscillators require clean power for optimal phase noise performance:
1. **Use LDO regulator**: Switching regulators introduce too much noise
2. **Decoupling capacitors**: Place 100nF ceramic + 10uF bulk close to VDD pin
3. **Power supply filtering**: Add ferrite bead or RC filter for sensitive applications
4. **Grounding**: Use star grounding, avoid ground loops
### Thermal Management
For TCXO and OCXO:
1. **Keep away from heat sources**: Position away from power regulators, processors
2. **Adequate airflow**: Ensure natural convection cooling
3. **Thermal isolation**: For OCXO, consider thermal isolation from PCB
4. **Temperature gradients**: Avoid temperature gradients across the oscillator
## Troubleshooting Guide
### Crystal Not Oscillating
1. **Check load capacitors**: Verify correct values for crystal CL specification
2. **Measure ESR**: Crystal ESR may be too high for oscillator circuit
3. **Check drive level**: Excessive drive can prevent startup
4. **Verify PCB layout**: Long traces or poor grounding can prevent oscillation
### Frequency Offset
1. **Adjust load capacitors**: Increase capacitors to lower frequency, decrease to raise
2. **Check stray capacitance**: PCB layout may have more stray capacitance than expected
3. **Verify crystal specification**: Ensure crystal matches design frequency
### High Phase Noise
1. **Improve power supply**: Add filtering, use LDO instead of switching regulator
2. **Check grounding**: Implement better ground plane and star grounding
3. **Isolate from noise sources**: Keep away from switching regulators and digital signals

faeInsights.content: Over my 12 years supporting crystal and oscillator designs, I've seen the same issues repeatedly. The most common problem is incorrect load capacitance - either wrong capacitor values or not accounting for stray capacitance. I always recommend measuring the actual oscillation frequency and adjusting capacitors accordingly. Another frequent issue is poor PCB layout - crystals are sensitive circuits that need proper grounding and isolation. I recommend keeping crystal traces under 20mm and using a solid ground plane. For oscillators, power supply noise is the biggest enemy of phase noise performance. Always use a clean LDO with good decoupling. For TCXO designs, thermal management is critical - keep the oscillator away from heat sources and allow adequate airflow. Following these guidelines will save you significant debug time.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Proper crystal design requires attention to load capacitance, PCB layout, and power supply quality. Most issues can be prevented by following established design guidelines.
keyTakeaways: Calculate load capacitance including stray capacitance
Keep crystal traces short and symmetric
Use clean LDO power for oscillators
Validate frequency and phase noise in testing
commonPitfalls: Noisy power supply affecting phase noise
bestPractices: Measure actual frequency and adjust capacitors
Use NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors for stability
Implement star grounding for crystal circuit
Keep crystals away from switching regulators
Validate phase noise in final design

faqs.question: How do I calculate the correct load capacitors for my crystal?
faqs.answer: Use the formula: CL = (C1 × C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. For example, with a 12pF crystal and 4pF stray capacitance: Required effective capacitance = 12 - 4 = 8pF. Using equal capacitors: 8 = C/2, so C = 16pF. Use standard 18pF capacitors. Always verify actual frequency and adjust if needed. Use NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors for temperature stability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on crystal CL and estimated stray capacitance, then verify and adjust.

faqs.question: Why is my crystal oscillating at the wrong frequency?
faqs.answer: Frequency offset is usually caused by incorrect load capacitance. If frequency is high, increase load capacitors. If frequency is low, decrease load capacitors. Typical adjustment: 1pF change affects frequency by 10-20ppm. Other causes: Wrong crystal specification, excessive stray capacitance from PCB layout, or temperature effects. Measure frequency with a high-impedance frequency counter and adjust capacitors iteratively until frequency is within specification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Adjust load capacitors - increase to lower frequency, decrease to raise frequency.

faqs.question: What is the maximum trace length for crystal connections?
faqs.answer: Keep crystal traces as short as possible - ideally under 20mm (0.8 inch). Longer traces increase stray capacitance and can cause: 1) Frequency shift from added capacitance. 2) Reduced oscillation margin. 3) Increased susceptibility to noise. 4) Potential oscillation failure. If longer traces are unavoidable, reduce load capacitor values to compensate for increased stray capacitance. Use ground plane under traces to reduce coupling. Match trace lengths for both crystal pins to maintain symmetry.
faqs.decisionGuide: Keep crystal traces under 20mm. Compensate with reduced load capacitors if longer traces are necessary.

faqs.question: How do I minimize phase noise in my oscillator circuit?
faqs.answer: To minimize phase noise: 1) Use clean LDO power supply with high PSRR (>60dB at 1kHz). 2) Implement proper decoupling - 100nF ceramic + 10uF tantalum close to VDD. 3) Use star grounding with solid ground plane. 4) Keep oscillator away from switching regulators and digital signals. 5) Use shielding or ground ring around oscillator for sensitive applications. 6) Ensure adequate power supply filtering. Phase noise is critical for RF and communication applications. Measure phase noise with a spectrum analyzer to validate your design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use clean LDO power, proper decoupling, and good grounding to minimize phase noise.

faqs.question: Should I use a crystal resonator or crystal oscillator?
faqs.answer: Choose based on your requirements: Crystal Resonator (passive): Lower cost, lower power, requires external oscillator circuit, suitable for most MCU applications. Crystal Oscillator (active): Guaranteed startup, better stability, higher cost, higher power, easier design. Use resonator when: Cost is critical, MCU has built-in oscillator, power consumption matters. Use oscillator when: Guaranteed startup is required, better stability needed, driving multiple loads, or external oscillator circuit not available. For most microcontroller applications, crystal resonators work well. For precision timing or challenging environments, use oscillators.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use resonators for cost-sensitive MCU applications. Use oscillators for guaranteed startup or precision timing.

customerCases.challenge: Crystal oscillator failing to start reliably in high-temperature environment
customerCases.solution: Implemented proper load capacitance calculation and improved PCB layout with shorter traces
customerCases.feedback: Oscillator now starts reliably across full temperature range. Design guidelines were very helpful.

customerCases.challenge: High phase noise affecting GPS sensitivity
customerCases.solution: Redesigned power supply with LDO and improved grounding per design guidelines
customerCases.feedback: Phase noise improved by 15dB. GPS sensitivity and acquisition time significantly improved.

faqs.question: What support does BeiLuo provide for ecec products?
faqs.answer: As an authorized ecec distributor, BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support including product selection guidance, application design review, and troubleshooting assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo FAE team for technical assistance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right ecec product?
faqs.answer: Follow the selection criteria in this guide based on your application requirements. Contact BeiLuo FAEs for personalized recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use this guide for selection, contact FAE for recommendations.

faqs.question: Where can I find additional technical resources?
faqs.answer: Additional technical resources including datasheets, application notes, and reference designs are available from BeiLuo. Contact our FAE team for access.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo for comprehensive technical resources.

faqs.question: Does BeiLuo provide design review services?
faqs.answer: Yes, BeiLuo provides design review services to help optimize your application. Contact our FAE team to schedule a review.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo FAEs for design review services.

faqs.question: How can I request samples?
faqs.answer: Contact BeiLuo sales team to request samples for evaluation. We typically ship samples within 1-2 business days.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo sales for sample requests.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a crystal resonator and a crystal oscillator?
faqs.answer: A crystal resonator is a passive component that requires an external oscillator circuit. A crystal oscillator is a complete module with integrated crystal and oscillator circuit. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use resonators for cost-sensitive applications. Use oscillators for guaranteed performance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right frequency tolerance for my application?
faqs.answer: ±30-50ppm is sufficient for general microcontroller clocks. ±20-30ppm for USB and standard communication. ±2.5-10ppm for cellular and GPS. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider both initial tolerance and stability over temperature.

faqs.question: What is load capacitance and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Load capacitance is the effective capacitance seen by the crystal in the oscillator circuit. Operating at a different CL causes frequency offset. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate CL using: CL = (C1 x C2)/(C1 + C2) + Cstray, where Cstray is typically 2-5pF.

faqs.question: What is the typical lead time for ECEC timing components?
faqs.answer: Crystal resonators 4-6 weeks, Crystal oscillators 6-8 weeks, TCXOs 8-10 weeks. We maintain stock of popular frequencies for faster delivery. For more detailed information and application guidance, please consult the product datasheet or contact our technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for current stock availability and expedited delivery options.

faqs.question: What is the maximum operating temperature for ECEC timing components?
faqs.answer: Commercial grade 0°C to +70°C, Industrial grade -40°C to +85°C, Automotive grade -40°C to +125°C or +150°C. Always select a temperature grade that provides margin above your expected operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always verify the temperature rating matches your application requirements and provides adequate margin.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the correct load capacitance for my crystal circuit?
faqs.answer: Load capacitance is calculated using the formula: CL = (C1 × C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray, where C1 and C2 are the external load capacitors and Cstray is the stray capacitance from PCB traces and IC pins (typically 2-5pF). For symmetric circuits where C1 = C2, this simplifies to CL = C1/2 + Cstray.
faqs.decisionGuide: Account for stray capacitance of 2-5pF when calculating load capacitor values to ensure accurate frequency operation.

faqs.question: When should I use a TCXO instead of a standard crystal oscillator?
faqs.answer: Use a TCXO when your application requires better than ±20-50ppm frequency stability over temperature. TCXOs are essential for GPS receivers, cellular communication systems, precision timing applications, and any system operating over a wide temperature range where frequency accuracy is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose TCXO for GPS, cellular, and precision timing. Standard oscillators are sufficient for general microcontroller clocks.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of MEMS oscillators over quartz crystals?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillators offer several advantages: 1) Superior shock resistance (50,000g+ vs 5,000g for quartz). 2) Programmable frequencies for design flexibility. 3) Faster startup time (<1ms vs 1-10ms). 4) No activity dips in frequency vs temperature curve. 5) Smaller package sizes available. 6) Better long-term stability and aging characteristics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose MEMS for high-shock applications, programmable frequencies, or when supply chain flexibility is important.
